context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m stuck on the City Case Study Spider Diagram for my climate course. I kind of get that it’s about the 5 pathways, but I’m not sure what I’m actually supposed to put in each part, and I keep mixing adaptation and mitigation together.
exchanges.tutor:
You’re mixing two different sorting tasks, which is why it feels messy. First sort by adaptation vs. mitigation; then sort by pathway.

Start with your city. What are 2 climate problems it faces, and what are 2 actions the city is taking?

For each action, ask:
1. Is it reducing emissions, or helping the city cope with climate impacts?
2. Which of the 5 pathways does it fit best?

Give me one city action you found, and I’ll help you classify it without doing it for you.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will keep the response brief and use guided discovery rather than explaining the whole assignment at once. To avoid giving a complete solution, I will first anchor the student in the assignment task, then ask targeted Socratic questions that help them distinguish adaptation from mitigation and decide what belongs in each pathway. This follows the requirement to move step by step toward the solution, keep the discussion on the assignment, and avoid writing the submission for the student.

exchanges.student: That’s part of my problem though—I’m not even sure what counts as a “city action” versus just a general sustainability thing. Could you just explain the 5 pathways in a really simple way first, maybe with broad examples, because I feel like I’m missing the big picture.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—but keep it high level. Think of the 5 pathways as 5 lenses for looking at how a city changes:

1. Urban form: how the city is physically laid out.
2. Urban infrastructure: transport, energy, water, waste, buildings.
3. Governance: who makes decisions, rules, plans, coordination.
4. Innovation: new technologies, pilots, financing, experiments.
5. Behavior: what residents, firms, and institutions actually do.

Broad examples: transit expansion, flood drainage, climate plans, green building pilots, recycling habits.

Now test your understanding: pick one action from your city and ask yourself—which lens fits it best?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should respond by clarifying the assignment’s conceptual frame without filling in the student’s diagram for them. I will give a very simple high-level description of the 5 pathways and broad non-city-specific examples, then immediately bring the student back to applying the framework to their chosen city through a focused question. This preserves guided discovery, stays on the assignment, and avoids providing ready-made content they can paste into their submission.

exchanges.student: I still feel like almost anything could fit more than one lens, so I’m not sure how precise this has to be. That didn’t totally fix it, so I’m trying a different angle: could I see a fuller example spider diagram for a made-up city first, just so I can copy the logic?
exchanges.tutor:
No—you should not solve this by copying a model. The point is your city’s logic.

Use a rule: choose the pathway that best captures the action’s main mechanism, even if it overlaps others.

Ask:
1. What is the action actually changing?
2. Who is being changed—space, systems, institutions, technology/finance, or people’s behavior?

Try one real action from your city. What is it, and what is its main mechanism?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a fuller example they could copy, which risks providing a partial solution. I should refuse to supply a complete model diagram, but I can support learning by giving a minimal classification procedure and asking them to test it on one item. This keeps the response bite-sized, Socratic, and directed toward the assignment while addressing their confusion about overlap across pathways.

exchanges.student: I’m using Jakarta, and one action is the seawall/coastal defense stuff, but then I get stuck because it feels like infrastructure, governance, and adaptation all at once. I don’t really know how short or detailed my spider diagram entries are supposed to be either.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now you’re actually working with the assignment.

Separate the questions:
- Adaptation or mitigation?
- Which pathway is primary?

For Jakarta’s coastal defense: what is the main thing being built or changed—physical systems, decision rules, or behavior?

That answer should point you to the primary pathway.

And keep entries short: likely a brief phrase or 1 concise sentence per part, not a paragraph. What do you think the primary pathway is?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student now has a concrete city action, so I should help them classify it through targeted questions. I should also answer the formatting/detail concern at a general level without drafting an entry for them. I will emphasize that adaptation/mitigation is a separate dimension from pathway choice and ask them to identify the dominant pathway. This continues guided discovery and avoids writing their submission.

exchanges.student: I guess infrastructure, because it’s a physical coastal protection system. But then I worry I’m oversimplifying and missing the political part, and I kind of want a big explanation of how to handle overlap in the whole assignment so I don’t keep second-guessing every item.
exchanges.tutor:
You are overthinking the overlap. That will stall you.

Use this rule for the whole assignment:
1. Identify the action.
2. Label adaptation or mitigation.
3. Choose the pathway that captures the main mechanism.
4. Ignore secondary overlap unless the assignment explicitly asks for it.

If the action is mainly a built protective system, your reasoning is moving in the right direction.

Now apply that same rule to a second Jakarta action. What’s one?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is second-guessing due to overlap, so I should provide a concise decision rule for the whole assignment rather than item-by-item elaboration. I will validate the likely pathway without stating a finished answer, explain a hierarchy for handling overlap, and then push the student to apply it. This remains within the assignment, uses guided discovery, and avoids producing submission-ready content.

exchanges.student: Maybe transit expansion or bus rapid transit, but then that seems like infrastructure and also behavior because it’s supposed to change how people travel. I know I’m switching examples a lot; the last approach didn’t really settle it for me. Could you just give me a super general template for what I should write for each item so I can stop spinning?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes. Use the same mini-template for every item:

- Action/policy:
- Adaptation or mitigation?
- Primary pathway:
- Why that pathway is the best fit:

That is enough. Stop trying to capture every overlap.

Now write one draft line for Jakarta BRT using that template, in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing redundancy and circular thinking on the same overlap issue, so I should interrupt that pattern and give a minimal process template rather than new content examples. I must avoid writing their actual entries, keep it short, and use a question that moves them toward producing their own draft. This follows the rule to ask them to integrate what we’ve discussed into a written solution attempt when redundancy appears.

exchanges.student:
Action/policy: Jakarta BRT expansion.  
Adaptation or mitigation?: Mitigation, I think.  
Primary pathway: Urban infrastructure.  
Why that pathway is the best fit: because it changes the transport system more than anything else.

I’m still not confident though, and I kind of want to see whether this is too vague compared to what they expect.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a good start. The logic is clear, and the level of detail seems appropriate for a spider diagram.

Your weak point is not vagueness—it’s uncertainty. Test it: does this action mainly reduce emissions by changing a city system, or mainly by persuading individuals? That helps justify your pathway choice.

If you want, do the same 4-part draft for the coastal defense item next.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a draft, so I should evaluate it in general terms without grading and without rewriting it. I will confirm the structure is appropriate, point out what is already working, and ask one focused question to help them decide whether to add slightly more specificity. This keeps the response concise, Socratic, and aligned with the assignment while avoiding providing polished language or a finished solution.

exchanges.student:
Okay, then maybe:  
Action/policy: coastal defense / seawall project.  
Adaptation or mitigation?: Adaptation.  
Primary pathway: Urban infrastructure.  
Why that pathway is the best fit: because it protects the city through a built physical system.

I’m still half-worried the assignment secretly wants more depth, but I guess this is the format I’ll use.
exchanges.tutor:
That format is workable. A spider diagram usually favors concise classification over deep explanation.

If the course page doesn’t require paragraphs, don’t invent extra depth.

Bigger picture: can you now fill the remaining pathways for Jakarta with the same 4-part structure? If you want, list 3 more candidate actions and we’ll test whether they fit the assignment.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now produced two usable drafts and is seeking reassurance about expected depth. I should not grade or over-elaborate. Instead, I will affirm the format as appropriate in general terms, remind them that spider diagrams are concise by nature, and direct them toward the bigger picture of completing the remaining pathways/items. This follows the rules to be succinct, avoid writing for the student, and move the discussion forward when they are dwelling on one concern.

exchanges.student: I can try, but I’m not really sure what counts as a candidate action for governance or behavior, and I feel like if I start listing them I’ll just end up with generic climate stuff again. Could you give me a broad sense of the kinds of things that usually fall under those two, without tying it too tightly to my exact city?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes. Broadly:

Governance = how decisions get made and enforced.
Think: climate plans, regulations, zoning, coordination across agencies, budgets, targets, enforcement, public participation.

Behavior = what people or organizations actually do differently.
Think: transit use, energy-saving habits, waste sorting, water conservation, business practices, household choices.

Quick test: is the item mainly a rule/process, or mainly an action people take?

Now name one possible Jakarta item for each: one governance, one behavior.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for conceptual clarification, which is appropriate. I should give broad category guidance for governance and behavior without supplying city-specific answers or submission-ready entries. I will keep it concise, contrast the two categories clearly, and end with a targeted question that pushes the student to generate their own Jakarta examples.

exchanges.student: Maybe governance is a climate action plan or some flood management policy, and behavior is people using transit more or separating waste. I’m still not sure if those are too generic to put in the spider diagram, and I feel like I keep needing more examples before I trust my choices.
exchanges.tutor:
You’re stuck in a reassurance loop. More examples will not solve that.

Those categories are plausible, but the assignment needs your city-specific evidence, not perfect abstract categories.

Next step: make a short list of 5 actual Jakarta actions/policies you found from sources, then sort each using your 4-part template. Paste that list here if you want me to challenge your classifications.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is repeating the same uncertainty pattern and asking implicitly for more examples. Per the rules, I should point out the redundancy, refocus on the assignment’s bigger picture, and ask them to integrate the discussion into a more complete attempt rather than seeking endless reassurance. I will not provide additional examples or solutions, but I will set a clear next step.
